speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Chaskel, you were going to tell us about the man who was manipulating the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was [INAUDIBLE] Goldberg was his name. He took over the list and he started things-- whoever paid him off, got out. He stretched out some people that worked for Schindler all the time from the beginning on. And he didn't care about. He took them off, and he put on some people that they pay him off. And sometimes he took money or diamonds. And he didn't even put them on, too. But the people from Madrich, he didn't touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was afraid, I guess, from Madrich. Because he knew that this list is not in his hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK, and now let's go forward in history. You were telling us about your family. And I wonder if you could tell us, again, your children's names and what they do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My oldest daughter's name is Aline. She is-- she went-- you want to know the schools, too?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she went to day schools. And then she went to the University of Illinois. And she had a master's degree in linguistic. And she married a rabbi by the name of Yolkut. And she has two children. One son is Danny. One is David. Danny is now in the Yeshiva University in-- in New York. He was on the March of Living one year in Poland. And he-- and he was one year in Israel studying. And now he's in-- in Yeshiva University in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what March of Living is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the March of the Living.

speaker: interviewee
text: March of the Living is like-- after the war, the Germans took the Jewish-- the people from the camps. And they took them-- it was called a march-- a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dying march, whatever they call it. There were people who were dying and these things-- march of death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, the Jewish people decided every year they'd take some-- some teenagers. And they take them to Poland. And they-- they call it March of Living. And they go to the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: They visit-- they visit all camps, Auschwitz and other ones like this. So he was one of them. And my son-in-law went with [INAUDIBLE] rabbis also to Poland. And he stopped in Plaszow. And they didn't know why he stopped in Plaszow. After this, he said that he stopped because his-- his-- his in-laws were there in Plaszow as-- as [INAUDIBLE]. And then we have the second daughter, Sabine. She was a-- she's a psychologist. She graduated from the University of Chicago. And her doctorate degree is from Ohio State University. And her husband has his doctor's degree from the University of Chicago. And he works for the government. She is a psychologist. He's a sociologist. They have three children. My daughter was one year as a psychologist in Poland also with a group of March of Living. And her daughter was also there. Not with her, but another group also of March of Living. Her second daughter is Elana. She's in high school now. And her youngest boy 11-- 10 years old. And he is in grammar school. They all live in-- in Silver Spring, Maryland. And my youngest-- our youngest daughter is Regine. She has her-- her degree from Northwestern University. She also attended day schools, Jewish day schools. And she has a degree from Northwestern University. And she is in-- in-- in-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in radio and news-- newscast. She is a-- she is a report-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a reporter anchorperson. Her husband is a-- is an attorney. He works for the Harris Bank as an-- as an attorney. She has three children, Jeremy, Rachel, and Ariela. They're all in-- in a day school. Ariela is the youngest. She is in-- in kindergarten. But the other ones are already in third grade and in fifth grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What have you tried to teach your children and your grandchildren about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never hide anything. We never hide anything, whatever they ask questions. They knew exactly when they were able to know. They knew that we were concentration camp even when they were small. But they didn't know what this means until they got a little older. And whenever they ask questions, we told them. They never hide anything from them. They knew everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do your grandchildren ask questions now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, our grandchildren know also. Because two of them were already on March of Living. So they-- naturally, they know. And the other ones knew already, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask you before they went on March of Living? Or, how did they tell you that they would be participating in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn that they were going into the March to the Living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. They told us that they were assigned to go. They were registered to go. And they go. So they let us know that they're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt very good about it, very good. I was very happy about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're speaking a lot these days and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we're speaking [INAUDIBLE] always tells me that they're very proud of us. They're very happy that we-- that we talk about and we let people know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you hoping will come from all your talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew-- from my experience, what I knew, I knew that a lot of people, they didn't know anything about. They know now. And especially the people that they claim that nothing happened, it was-- they lied. They say that's lie, that's a hoax. Now, they-- they think about more that it's true. They believe in it more I hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anything else that you want to add either about your story or just anything else you'd like to ask for your record?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing special. But I'm happy that I'm alive and I can tell the story. And I hope that it's going to do something good in the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So come right next to the camera. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Chaskel, would you like to introduce the people that you're standing with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Next to me is my lovely wife for 53 years. Next to her is our daughter Regine. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Our youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from left to right is Jeremy, Ariela, and Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name--

speaker: interviewee
text: From--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, from right to left.

speaker: interviewee
text: From left to right. From left to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, from right to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now--oh, OK, now it's right. OK. This was not on tape yet, huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes it is. Let's do it one more time.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. One more time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And use everybody's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can go-- you can point to people. Don't be afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Margot for 53 years. This is our daughter Regine. And in front of her, from right to left, is Jeremy, our grandson, Ariela, our youngest granddaughter, and Rachel, our granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Margot you said that there's something you'd like to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to say that I dedicate the whole-- my whole testimony I gave today to my grandchildren-- to my children, my grandchildren, and my, hopefully, someday my great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regine, you are here representing yourself and your sisters. Can you tell us a little bit about what you think your growing up experience was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that it was a real privilege being the daughters of Margot and Chaskel Schlesinger for a number of reasons. First of all, because they're very special people. Not only-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] despite their experiences, but also because of their experiences, I think that they always knew what was really important in life. They treasured family more than anything else. Everything was sacrificed. They-- they understood that family was the most important thing-- that if you lose that, you lose everything. They are the most morally rooted people that I know. I mean, they-- they would never do anything that they thought would hurt anyone else. And growing up, they were-- when we were growing up, they were always very open and honest with us about their experiences. And I think that made it easier for us to cope with it as opposed to some survivors who didn't want to tell their children, didn't want to impose the pain on their children. And I think their children may have ended up suffering more, because of it. And it's just a been a privilege to be their daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that their war experiences influenced you and your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think probably. I think there were a lot of things, subtle influences. I think the fact that we knew what they had been through and I think that we probably grew up being more outraged morally at things, injustices, and at things that were going on in the world than maybe other people. We were always more sensitive to that, I think, knowing what they had gone through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jeremy, would you like to say something about your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't really think of anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spend a lot of time with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kind of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you're going to watch this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: teach you anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: and pa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: You like [INAUDIBLE] with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stop!

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, what about you? What [INAUDIBLE] about grandma and grandpa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like mama, because she gives me food all the time. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: What she say?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gives her food all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gives her food all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah. [INAUDIBLE] to make paintings.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you're thinking, Ariela, did you want to say something about your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. I like her. She gives me Tic-Tacs and gum.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did she say?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave me Tic-Tacs and gum.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. Regine, what do you hope that your children will eventually come to learn from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, the same things that I learned from my parents. I mean, I think a sense of right and wrong, the kinds of values that my parents instilled in me and my sisters. I hope that that will be passed along first through them and then eventually through me as well. And just-- that same kind of-- those priorities in life that I think my parents have that so many people kind of lose sight of and that I want my children to have. Priorities of family, of Jewish life, of morality. And I think they've given those things to me. And I hope that they give them to my children as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chaskel, this is your tape. You get the last word. What would you like to say to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my family. I'm very proud of my family. And I couldn't ask for anything better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time you can begin. Tell us about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it, hold it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one page of Schindler's list. My name is under number 37. My name is Schlesinger Chaskel Abraham. For some reason-- Abraham is my middle name. For some reason, they have only the middle name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you may begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is a document. I didn't want to leave the camp without any documents. So they gave me from the camp, they gave me this document. This is in the Czech language. And here's the headline of the-- from the company. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Oskar Schindler, Krakau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which camp is this from?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Brunnlitz. We got it from Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, hold it still for me. I wasn't done. Flip it over one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it nice and still for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you may begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also a document in two languages-- Czech language and Polish language. Because we didn't have any other documents. So we insisted that we cannot leave without a document.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave us this document in two languages, for the Czech people and for the Polish people. When we arrived in Poland, we could have this in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little bit about what it says?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- yeah, this says that we are-- we were in concentrate by Schindler and we left the camp as free people. And this is the document that says that we are free people. We can go any place we want in Poland. We are Polish citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: This [INAUDIBLE] came back to from Poland. We went to the-- to the town of evidence office. And he got this document that we are-- with this picture that we are Polish citizens. And we are from Tarnow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Chaskel, what are we are looking it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we are looking at my brother's three children. On the left is the oldest son. His name has Henek. In Yiddish, he was called [YIDDISH]. And in the middle is the daughter Renee. And on the left side-- I mean, in the right side is Itzak, Itzak. They were three children from my brother Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody-- I don't remember who. Somebody gave me these pictures after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the right is my brother Moishe and his wife Sheva. They all perished. I mean, Sheva and the three children perished during the war. My brother survived, and he passed away here in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when these pictures were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: These pictures were taken before the war, must've been in 1935 or '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tarnow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, stand by. And go ahead. Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side is my brother Chaim. He was five years older than I am. He was single. He perished by Hitler. In the middle is my sister-in-law, Sheva, Moishe's, my brother's wife. She also perished during the war. And on the right side is myself, Chaskel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1937 in [INAUDIBLE]. This was a resort. We were on vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE] you can look at the screen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, wait. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Chaskel. Who was in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, several friends of ours and some relatives. Some of these people came back from Russia. And standing is myself. Next is a cousin of Margot. Her name is Mala Roferge. And next to him is Getzler. And next to him is Meyer Roferge. And sitting from this side is--

speaker: interviewer
text: From your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? From your left side-- from my left side--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is Izzy Schrieber. He came back from Russia. Next is my wife Margot. And next to Margot is Kalmer Roferge, which is the husband of this women Mala Roferge. And then the last one is a friend of ours, Leibish Aronberg. Also from Russia. Most of them came back from Russia after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they went by themselves to the Russia part of-- when the Russian occupied part of Poland, and then the Russians sent them back to Siberia, to deep Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what year was this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland back when they came back from Russia, they didn't have anywhere to go. So they went to our apartment. And we got together, and we took these pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tarnow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture-- all five people in this picture were on Schindler's list. From left to right is the name Chaim Seigel. As a matter of fact, he was my partner in Paris after the war manufacturing from shirts. Next to him is Margot, then I. And this was a couple. They worked for Madrich also. They were personal tailors for the Germans. At that time, they weren't married. That was-- her name was [INAUDIBLE]. And his name was Lieberman. After the war, they got married. But they worked from Madrich. And then they came-- they were on Schindler's list also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Paris near the-- the Eiffel Tower.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this picture was taken on an airport, a midway airport when we arrived America-- in Chicago. The one on the left first is Margot's cousin. Her name is Helen Sloan. She was American. She was born in America. She came to see us. Next is myself with-- with Regine in my arms. Next is Margot's father Julius, Julius Wind. Next to him is Margot. And next to Margot is her brother-- oldest brother Eddie. And on the bottom of Eddie is Aline and Sabine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1953 when we arrived in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is when our grandson was born. And that was the day of the bris. From left to right, myself. The next one is our-- our son-in-law Martin Yolkut. His son was born. That was the birth of his son. Next to him is our son-in-law Chaim Himmelfarb. And next to Chaim Himmelfarb is my brother Moishe. We all were waiting for the mohel to come for the bris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in the synagogue in-- in Lincolnwood.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1975.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken our second granddaughter's bat mizva-- Elana Himmelfarb. Standing from left to right is Stuart Meisel. Next, myself. Then comes our grandson Chaim Himmelfarb, the father of the bat mizvah. Next to him is bat mitzvah Elana Himmelfarb. Next to her is David Yolkut, her cousin our son-- grandson. Next to him is Danny Yolkut, his-- her cousin and our grandson. And next is his father, rabbi Martin Yolkut. Sitting from right to left is Aline Yolkut, our daughter. Next to her is Yonit Himmelfarb, the sister from the bat mitzvah. Next to her is Eli Himmelfarb, the brother. And next to him is his mother, our daughter [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] our daughter Sabine Himmelfarb. Next to her is my wife Margot and Elana-- Ariela Meisel. In the back is standing Jeremy Meisel. This woman is my sister-in-law, Margot's brother's wife by herself because her husband died. Next to her is Regine and her daughter Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] must have been two years ago. Must've been in '92, maybe. '92 I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In city-- in Washington. They live in Silver Spring, but this-- this party was in Washington DC.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in the last year with Keneally, the author of the book From the right to left, myself, Margot, Keneally, our daughter Regine and her husband Stuart Meisel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was take--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken of Governor Edgar. That was an occasion because Edgar put in this, all the schools should have see the movie from Schindler and should read the book from Schindler. So it was taken-- we were there. From left to right is Governor Edgar, his wife and-- what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brenda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brenda and my wife and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by please. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a piece. It's called [INAUDIBLE] [? box. ?] This is for religious purposes. And this is a very old thing that's in the family maybe for 150 years. And that was very precious to me. And I was hiding this during the war. After the war, we dig it out and we got it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is it sitting in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's called [INAUDIBLE] box.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it sitting on?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's sitting on a tablecloth, which was also to me very, very valuable. That's why we saved it. And thank god we got it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were these things hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hidden under the floor in apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tarnow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's so fast. He wants to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is, I tell you. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This silver candlestick is also from the family. And it was very precious to us. So we also was hiding this. And we got it back after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you get it back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it back in 1945 when I came back from the camp.
